Plain English Description:
Measures how much the lower leg swings relative to the rider’s hip over time.
A stable lower leg is a core indicator of rider balance and seat stability.
Formula:
LowerLegStability = RMS( v_leg(t) - mean(v_leg) ) / torso_length

v_leg(t) = A(t) - H(t) [ankle position relative to hip over time]
Symbol Definitions:
A = ankle position
H = hip position
K = knee position
Heel = heel position
T = toe position

baseline = reference neutral position
torso_length = distance from midpoint of shoulders to midpoint of hips
foot_length = distance from heel to toe
Feedback Mapping:
0 – 0.03 → Excellent lower leg stability
0.03 – 0.06 → Very stable leg
0.06 – 0.10 → Slight leg movement
0.10 – 0.15 → Leg instability visible

Plain English Description:
Measures how consistently the rider maintains knee bend during the ride.
Frequent changes in knee angle often indicate gripping or loss of balance.
Formula:
KneeAngleStability = StdDev( theta_knee(t) )

theta_knee(t) = angle(H(t), K(t), A(t)) [knee angle over time]
Feedback Mapping:
0 – 3° → Knee very stable
3 – 6° → Knee mostly stable
6 – 10° → Mild knee movement
10 – 18° → Noticeable instability

Plain English Description:
Measures how stable the rider’s heel position is during the ride.
Stable heels help anchor the rider’s leg and maintain balance.
Formula:
HeelPositionStability = StdDev( theta_heel(t) )

theta_heel(t) = angle(K(t), A(t), T(t)) [ankle-foot angle over time]
Feedback Mapping:
0 – 4° → Heel extremely stable
4 – 7° → Heel mostly stable
7 – 12° → Heel movement noticeable
12 – 18° → Heel unstable

Plain English Description: Detects whether the rider’s leg gradually shifts forward or backward relative to the seat.
Formula:
LowerLegDrift = | mean(v_leg(t)) - baseline_leg_position |

v_leg(t) = A(t) - H(t) [ankle position relative to hip over time]
Feedback Mapping:
0 – 0.02 → Leg position ideal
0.02 – 0.05 → Slight drift
0.05 – 0.09 → Moderate drift
0.09 – 0.12 → Leg creeping forward/back

Plain English Description: Measures whether the rider’s hands are carried at even heights. Uneven hands can contribute to crookedness and uneven contact
Formula:
ReinSymmetry = | y(LW) - y(RW) | / shoulder_width

LW = mean(LW(t)) [average left wrist position over the analysis window]
RW = mean(RW(t)) [average right wrist position over the analysis window]
Symbol Definitions:
LW(t) = left wrist position at time t
RW(t) = right wrist position at time t
LS(t) = left shoulder position at time t
RS(t) = right shoulder position at time t
LE(t) = left elbow position at time t
RE(t) = right elbow position at time t
W(t) = wrist position at time t
S(t) = shoulder position at time t
E(t) = elbow position at time t

shoulder_width = distance between left and right shoulders
torso_length = distance from midpoint of shoulders to midpoint of hips
Feedback Mapping:
0 – 0.02 → Hands perfectly even
0.02 – 0.05 → Hands mostly level
0.05 – 0.08 → Slight hand imbalance
0.08 – 0.15 → Noticeable uneven hands

Plain English Description: Measures how much the rider’s hands move relative to the shoulders over time. Steadier hands generally support more consistent contact.
Formula:
ReinSteadiness = RMS( v_hand(t) - mean(v_hand) ) / torso_length

v_hand(t) = W(t) - S(t) [wrist position relative to shoulder over time]
Feedback Mapping:
0 – 0.02 → Hands extremely steady
0.02 – 0.05 → Hands steady
0.05 – 0.08 → Slight hand movement
0.08 – 0.15 → Hands moving frequently

Plain English Description: Measures whether the distance between the hands remains consistent over time.
Formula:
HandSpacingStability = StdDev( d_hands(t) / shoulder_width )

d_hands(t) = distance(LW(t), RW(t)) [distance between hands over time]
Feedback Mapping:
0.00–0.02 = hand width very consistent
0.02–0.04 = mostly consistent
0.04–0.06 = mild inconsistency
0.06–0.10 = noticeably variable

Plain English Description: Measures whether both elbows maintain similar bend angles.
Formula:
ElbowSymmetry = | theta_elbow_left - theta_elbow_right |

theta_elbow_left = angle(LS, LE, LW) [left elbow angle]
theta_elbow_right = angle(RS, RE, RW) [right elbow angle]
Feedback Mapping:
0–3° = elbows very even
3–6° = mostly even
6–10° = mild asymmetry
10–18° = noticeable asymmetry

Plain English Description: Measures whether the elbow maintains a soft, functional bend rather than becoming rigid or excessively unstable.
Formula:
ElbowElasticity_mean = mean( theta_elbow(t) )
ElbowElasticity_var = StdDev( theta_elbow(t) )

theta_elbow(t) = angle(S(t), E(t), W(t)) [elbow angle over time]
Typical values:
Mean angle: roughly ~120° to 170°
Variation: 0° to ~25°
Ideal Value:
Mean angle target band: ~130°–155°
Variation target: ~0°–8°

Target range for mean; lower is better for variability
Feedback Mapping:
Mean too open + low variability = likely locked elbow
Mean in target band + low variability = elastic and steady
High variability = unstable elbow position

Plain English Description: Measures whether the rider leans forward or backward relative to vertical.
Formula:
UpperBodyVerticalAlignment = mean( theta_trunk(t) )

theta_trunk(t) = angle( trunk_vector(t), vertical ) [trunk angle relative to vertical over time]
trunk_vector(t) = MS(t) - MH(t) [vector from mid-hip to mid-shoulder]
Symbol Definitions:
MS(t) = midpoint between shoulders at time t
MH(t) = midpoint between hips at time t
LH(t) = left hip position at time t
RH(t) = right hip position at time t
Head(t) = head position at time t

vertical = vertical axis of the image
torso_length = distance from midpoint of shoulders to midpoint of hips
Feedback Mapping:
0 – 5° → Excellent posture
5 – 10° → Good alignment
10 – 15° → Slight lean
15 – 25° → Noticeable lean

Plain English Description: Detects whether the rider collapses or leans to one side.
Formula:
ShoulderLevelness = | y(LS) - y(RS) | / shoulder_width

LS = mean(LS(t)) [average left shoulder position over the analysis window]
RS = mean(RS(t)) [average right shoulder position over the analysis window]
shoulder_width = distance(LS, RS) [distance between average left and right shoulder positions]
Feedback Mapping:
0 – 0.02 → Shoulders level
0.02 – 0.05 → Minor tilt
0.05 – 0.08 → Slight lean
0.08 – 0.15 → Noticeable tilt

Plain English Description: Measures twisting through the torso by comparing shoulder-line orientation to hip-line orientation.
Formula:
TorsoRotation = | yaw(shoulder_line) - yaw(hip_line) |

shoulder_line = mean(LS(t) - RS(t)) [average shoulder line orientation over the analysis window]
hip_line = mean(LH(t) - RH(t)) [average hip line orientation over the analysis window]
yaw(...) = horizontal orientation angle
Feedback Mapping:
0–5° = torso aligned
5–10° = slight twist
10–15° = noticeable twist
15–25° = strong twist

Plain English Description: Measures how much the rider’s head moves or bounces relative to its average position.
Formula: HeadStability = RMS( Head(t) - mean(Head) ) / torso_length
Feedback Mapping:
0.00–0.02 = head very stable
0.02–0.04 = mostly steady
0.04–0.07 = mild movement
0.07–0.12 = noticeable head movement

Plain English Description: Measures stability of the rider’s torso relative to the pelvis.
Formula:
CoreStability = RMS( trunk_vector(t) - mean(trunk_vector) ) / torso_length

trunk_vector(t) = MS(t) - MH(t) [vector from mid-hip to mid-shoulder over time]
Symbol Definitions:
MS(t) = midpoint between shoulders at time t
MH(t) = midpoint between hips at time t
LH(t) = left hip position at time t
RH(t) = right hip position at time t

vertical = vertical axis of the image
torso_length = distance from midpoint of shoulders to midpoint of hips
hip_width = distance between left and right hips
Feedback Mapping:
0 – 0.015 → Excellent core stability
0.015 – 0.035 → Good stability
0.035 – 0.07 → Slight instability
0.07 – 0.12 → Core unstable

Plain English Description: Measures how consistently the rider maintains trunk angle over time.
Formula:
TrunkAngleStability = StdDev( theta_trunk(t) )

theta_trunk(t) = angle( MS(t) - MH(t), vertical ) [trunk angle relative to vertical over time]
Feedback Mapping:
0–2° = extremely stable trunk
2–4° = stable trunk posture
4–7° = mild variation
7–12° = noticeable variation

Plain English Description: Measures how smoothly the pelvis follows the horse’s movement.
Formula:
PelvisVerticalStability = StdDev( y(midHip(t)) ) / torso_length

midHip(t) = ( LH(t) + RH(t) ) / 2 [midpoint between left and right hips over time]
Ideal Value:
0.01 – 0.03

 (This is one of the few metrics where the ideal is not zero, because the pelvis should move with the horse.)
Feedback Mapping:
0.01 – 0.03 → Excellent seat following motion
0.03 – 0.05 → Smooth seat
0.05 – 0.08 → Slight bounce
0.08 – 0.12 → Noticeable bounce

Plain English Description: Measures whether one hip drops or lifts more than the other.
Formula:
PelvisLevelness = | y(LH) - y(RH) | / hip_width

LH = mean(LH(t)) [average left hip position over the analysis window]
RH = mean(RH(t)) [average right hip position over the analysis window]
Feedback Mapping:
0.00–0.02 = pelvis level
0.02–0.05 = slight asymmetry
0.05–0.08 = mild collapse on one side
0.08–0.15 = noticeable unevenness

Plain English Description: Measures whether the heel is lower than the toe. This is the primary metric for the trainer cue: “heels down.”
Formula:
HeelDepth = ( y(T) - y(Heel) ) / foot_length

T = mean(T(t)) [average toe position over the analysis window]
Heel = mean(Heel(t)) [average heel position over the analysis window]
Symbol Definitions:
Heel(t) = heel position at time t
T(t) = toe position at time t
A(t) = ankle position at time t
K(t) = knee position at time t

foot_length = distance from heel to toe
Feedback Mapping:
<0.00 = heel above toe
0.00–0.05 = nearly level
0.05–0.20 = heel appropriately down
0.20–0.30 = strong heel-down position

Plain English Description: Measures ankle articulation and overall foot organization.
Formula:
AnkleFlexionAngle = angle(K, A, T)

K = mean(K(t)) [average knee position over the analysis window]
A = mean(A(t)) [average ankle position over the analysis window]
T = mean(T(t)) [average toe position over the analysis window]
Ideal Value: Target band rather than single value; depends on discipline and situation
Feedback Mapping: Needs calibration against riding context; better used as an internal metric before direct user-facing feedback

Plain English Description: Measures whether the foot rotates or swings inconsistently over time.
Formula:
FootStability = StdDev( phi_foot(t) )

phi_foot(t) = angle(Heel(t), T(t)) [foot orientation angle over time]
Feedback Mapping:
0–3° = very stable foot
3–6° = mostly stable
6–10° = mild instability
10–18° = noticeable movement

Plain English Description: Measures whether rider stays centered over saddle.
Formula:
RiderCenterlineAlignment = | x(MS) - x(MH) | / torso_length

MS = mean(MS(t)) [average midpoint between shoulders over the analysis window]
MH = mean(MH(t)) [average midpoint between hips over the analysis window]
Symbol Definitions:
MS(t) = midpoint between shoulders at time t
MH(t) = midpoint between hips at time t
LS(t) = left shoulder position at time t
RS(t) = right shoulder position at time t
LH(t) = left hip position at time t
RH(t) = right hip position at time t
LK(t) = left knee position at time t
RK(t) = right knee position at time t
LA(t) = left ankle position at time t
RA(t) = right ankle position at time t

shoulder_width = distance between left and right shoulders
hip_width = distance between left and right hips
torso_length = distance from midpoint of shoulders to midpoint of hips
knee_angle_scale = normalization constant for knee asymmetry
Feedback Mapping:
0.00–0.02 = centered
0.02–0.05 = slightly off-center
0.05–0.08 = moderate offset
0.08–0.12 = noticeable imbalance

Plain English Description: Composite measure of body symmetry across shoulders, pelvis, and knees.
Formula:
LeftRightSymmetryIndex = mean( ShoulderLevelness, PelvisLevelness, knee_asymmetry_norm )

knee_asymmetry_norm = | theta_knee_left - theta_knee_right | / knee_angle_scale [normalized knee angle asymmetry]
theta_knee_left = angle(LH, LK, LA) [left knee angle]
theta_knee_right = angle(RH, RK, RA) [right knee angle]
LH, LK, LA = mean([LH or LK or LA](t)) [average left hip or knee or ankle position over the analysis window]
RH, RK, RA = mean([RH or RK or RA](t)) [average roght hip or knee or ankle position over the analysis window]
Feedback Mapping:
0.00–0.03 = highly symmetrical
0.03–0.06 = mostly symmetrical
0.06–0.10 = mild asymmetry
0.10–0.15 = noticeable asymmetry

Plain English Description: Measures whether the limbs move independently from the trunk rather than being dragged by torso instability.
Formula:
SeatIndependence = corr( trunk_motion(t), limb_motion(t) )

trunk_motion(t) = motion magnitude of the trunk over time
limb_motion(t) = motion magnitude of wrists and ankles over time
Typical values: -1 to +1 correlation, though in practice usually ~0 to 1 here
Ideal Value: Closer to 0 is generally better than closer to 1
Feedback Mapping:
0.00–0.20 = strong independence
0.20–0.40 = good independence
0.40–0.60 = moderate coupling
0.60–0.80 = strong coupling
